   334→      "root_cause": "The underlying technical reason — why it happened",
   335→      "takeaway": "The transferable lesson — what to do or avoid in similar situations, useful outside this project",
   336→      "applies_when": "Conditions under which this knowledge is relevant (framework version, configuration, etc.)",
   337→      "confidence": 80,
   338→      "evidence": ["User#7: quoted text...", "Assistant#8: quoted text..."]
   339→    }
   340→  ]
   341→}
   342→
   343→Only include insights rated 70+ confidence. If you cannot cite evidence, drop the insight. Return empty arrays for categories with no strong insights. Max 3 decisions, 5 learnings.
   344→Evidence should reference the labeled turns in the conversation (e.g., "User#2", "Assistant#5").
   345→
   346→Respond with valid JSON only, wrapped in <json>...</json> tags. Do not include any other text.`;
   347→}
   348→
   349→const VALID_SESSION_CHARACTERS = new Set<string>([
   350→  'deep_focus', 'bug_hunt', 'feature_build', 'exploration', 'refactor', 'learning', 'quick_task',
   351→]);
   352→
   353→export interface AnalysisResponse {
   354→  facets?: {
   355→    outcome_satisfaction: string;
   356→    workflow_pattern: string | null;
   357→    had_course_correction: boolean;
   358→    course_correction_reason: string | null;
   359→    iteration_count: number;
   360→    friction_points: Array<{
   361→      category: string;
   362→      attribution?: string;
   363→      description: string;
   364→      severity: string;
   365→      resolution: string;
   366→    }>;
   367→    effective_patterns: Array<{
   368→      category: string;
   369→      description: string;
   370→      confidence: number;
   371→    }>;
   372→  };
   373→  session_character?: SessionCharacter;
   374→  summary: {
   375→    title: string;
   376→    content: string;
   377→    outcome?: 'success' | 'partial' | 'abandoned' | 'blocked';
   378→    bullets: string[];
   379→  };
   380→  decisions: Array<{
   381→    title: string;
   382→    situation?: string;
   383→    choice?: string;
   384→    reasoning: string;
   385→    alternatives?: Array<{ option: string; rejected_because: string }>;
   386→    trade_offs?: string;
   387→    revisit_when?: string;
   388→    confidence?: number;
   389→    evidence?: string[];
   390→  }>;
   391→  learnings: Array<{
   392→    title: string;
   393→    symptom?: string;
   394→    root_cause?: string;
   395→    takeaway?: string;
   396→    applies_when?: string;
   397→    confidence?: number;
   398→    evidence?: string[];
   399→  }>;
   400→}
   401→
   402→export interface ParseError {
   403→  error_type: 'json_parse_error' | 'no_json_found' | 'invalid_structure';
   404→  error_message: string;
   405→  response_length: number;
   406→  response_preview: string;
   407→}
   408→
   409→function buildResponsePreview(text: string, head = 200, tail = 200): string {
   410→  if (text.length <= head + tail + 20) return text;
   411→  return `${text.slice(0, head)}\n...[${text.length - head - tail} chars omitted]...\n${text.slice(-tail)}`;
   412→}
   413→
   414→export type ParseResult<T> =
   415→  | { success: true; data: T }
   416→  | { success: false; error: ParseError };
   417→
   418→export function extractJsonPayload(response: string): string | null {
   419→  const tagged = response.match(/<json>\s*([\s\S]*?)\s*<\/json>/i);
   420→  if (tagged?.[1]) return tagged[1].trim();
   421→  const jsonMatch = response.match(/\{[\s\S]*\}/);
   422→  return jsonMatch ? jsonMatch[0] : null;
   423→}
   424→
   425→/**
   426→ * Parse the LLM response into structured insights.
   427→ */
   428→export function parseAnalysisResponse(response: string): ParseResult<AnalysisResponse> {
   429→  const response_length = response.length;
   430→
   431→  const preview = buildResponsePreview(response);
   432→
   433→  const jsonPayload = extractJsonPayload(response);
   434→  if (!jsonPayload) {
   435→    console.error('No JSON found in analysis response');
   436→    return {
   437→      success: false,
   438→      error: { error_type: 'no_json_found', error_message: 'No JSON found in analysis response', response_length, response_preview: preview },
   439→    };
   440→  }
   441→
   442→  let parsed: AnalysisResponse;
   443→  try {
   444→    parsed = JSON.parse(jsonPayload) as AnalysisResponse;
   445→  } catch {
   446→    // Attempt repair — handles trailing commas, unclosed braces, truncated output
   447→    try {
   448→      parsed = JSON.parse(jsonrepair(jsonPayload)) as AnalysisResponse;
   449→    } catch (err) {
   450→      const msg = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
   451→      console.error('Failed to parse analysis response (after jsonrepair):', err);
   452→      return {
   453→        success: false,
   454→        error: { error_type: 'json_parse_error', error_message: msg, response_length, response_preview: preview },
   455→      };
   456→    }
   457→  }
   458→
   459→  if (!parsed.summary || typeof parsed.summary.title !== 'string') {
   460→    console.error('Invalid analysis response structure');
   461→    return {
   462→      success: false,
   463→      error: { error_type: 'invalid_structure', error_message: 'Missing or invalid summary field', response_length, response_preview: preview },
   464→    };
   465→  }
   466→
   467→  parsed.decisions = parsed.decisions || [];
   468→  parsed.learnings = parsed.learnings || [];
   469→
   470→  // Validate session_character — drop if not a recognized value
   471→  if (parsed.session_character && !VALID_SESSION_CHARACTERS.has(parsed.session_character)) {
   472→    parsed.session_character = undefined;
   473→  }
   474→
   475→  // Observability: warn when LLM still uses "tooling-limitation".
   476→  // Monitors whether FRICTION_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE is working.
   477→  // Remove after confirming classification quality over ~20 new sessions.
   478→  if (parsed.facets?.friction_points?.some(fp => fp.category === 'tooling-limitation')) {
   479→    console.warn('[friction-monitor] LLM classified friction as "tooling-limitation" — verify this is a genuine tool limitation, not an agent/rate-limit/approach issue');
   480→  }
   481→
   482→  // Observability: warn when LLM returns effective_pattern without category field.
   483→  // Catches models that ignore the category instruction (especially smaller Ollama models).
   484→  // Remove after confirming classification quality over ~20 new sessions.
   485→  if (parsed.facets?.effective_patterns?.some(ep => !ep.category)) {
   486→    console.warn('[pattern-monitor] LLM returned effective_pattern without category field');
   487→  }
   488→
   489→  return { success: true, data: parsed };
   490→}
   491→
   492→/**
   493→ * Lightweight facet-only prompt for backfilling sessions that already have insights
   494→ * or for chunked sessions where facets can't be merged across chunks.
   495→ * Input: session summary + first/last 20 messages (~2.5k tokens).
   496→ * Output: facet JSON only (~350 tokens).
   497→ */
   498→export const FACET_ONLY_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are assessing an AI coding session to extract structured metadata for cross-session pattern analysis. You will receive a session summary and a sample of messages (first and last from the conversation).
   499→
   500→Extract session facets — a holistic assessment of how the session went:
   501→
   502→1. outcome_satisfaction: "high" (completed successfully), "medium" (partial), "low" (problems), "abandoned" (gave up)
   503→2. workflow_pattern: The dominant pattern, or null. Values: "plan-then-implement", "iterative-refinement", "debug-fix-verify", "explore-then-build", "direct-execution"
   504→3. friction_points: Up to 5 moments where progress stalled (array).
   505→   Each: { category (kebab-case, prefer: ${CANONICAL_FRICTION_CATEGORIES.join(', ')}), attribution ("user-actionable"|"ai-capability"|"environmental"), description (one neutral sentence with specific details), severity ("high"|"medium"|"low"), resolution ("resolved"|"workaround"|"unresolved") }
   506→${FRICTION_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE}
   507→4. effective_patterns: Up to 3 things that worked well (array).
   508→   Each: { category (kebab-case, prefer: ${CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES.join(', ')}), description (specific technique, 1-2 sentences), confidence (0-100) }
   509→${EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE}
   510→5. had_course_correction: true/false — did the user redirect the AI?
   511→6. course_correction_reason: Brief explanation if true, null otherwise
   512→7. iteration_count: How many user clarification/correction cycles occurred
   513→
   514→Respond with valid JSON only, wrapped in <json>...</json> tags.`;
   515→
   516→export function generateFacetOnlyPrompt(
   517→  projectName: string,
   518→  sessionSummary: string | null,
   519→  firstMessages: string,
   520→  lastMessages: string
   521→): string {
   522→  return `Assess this AI coding session and extract facets.
   523→
   524→Project: ${projectName}
   525→${sessionSummary ? `Session Summary: ${sessionSummary}\n` : ''}
   526→--- FIRST MESSAGES ---
   527→${firstMessages}
   528→--- END FIRST MESSAGES ---
   529→
   530→--- LAST MESSAGES ---
   531→${lastMessages}
   532→--- END LAST MESSAGES ---
   533→
   534→Extract facets in this JSON format:
   535→{
   536→  "outcome_satisfaction": "high | medium | low | abandoned",
   537→  "workflow_pattern": "string or null",
   538→  "had_course_correction": false,
   539→  "course_correction_reason": null,
   540→  "iteration_count": 0,
   541→  "friction_points": [],

   549→
   550→// --- Prompt Quality Analysis ---
   551→
   552→export const PROMPT_QUALITY_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are a prompt engineering coach helping developers communicate more effectively with AI coding assistants. You review conversations and identify specific moments where better prompting would have saved time.
   553→
   554→You will identify:
   555→1. **Wasted turns**: User messages that led to clarifications, corrections, or repeated instructions because the original prompt was unclear, missing context, or too vague.
   556→2. **Anti-patterns**: Recurring bad habits in the user's prompting style, with specific fixes.
   557→3. **Session traits**: Higher-level behavioral patterns about how the session was structured and managed.
   558→4. **Efficiency score**: A 0-100 rating of how optimally the user communicated.
   559→5. **Actionable tips**: Specific improvements the user can make.
   560→
   561→Before evaluating, mentally walk through the conversation and identify:
   562→1. Each time the assistant asked for clarification that could have been avoided
   563→2. Each time the user corrected the assistant's interpretation
   564→3. Each time the user repeated an instruction they gave earlier
   565→4. Whether the session covers too many unrelated objectives (context drift / session bloat)
   566→5. Whether the user provided critical context or requirements late that should have been mentioned upfront
   567→6. Whether the user discussed the plan/approach before jumping into implementation, or dove straight into code
   568→These are your candidate findings. Only include them if they are genuinely actionable.
   569→
   570→Guidelines:
   571→- Focus on USER messages only — don't critique the assistant's responses
   572→- A "wasted turn" is when the user had to send a follow-up message to clarify, correct, or repeat something that could have been included in the original prompt
   573→- Only mark a wasted turn if the assistant explicitly asked for clarification or corrected a misunderstanding
   574→- Be constructive, not judgmental — the goal is to help users improve
   575→- Consider the context: some clarification exchanges are normal and expected
   576→- A score of 100 means every user message was perfectly clear and complete
   577→- A score of 50 means about half the messages could have been more efficient
   578→
   579→Length Guidance:
   580→- Max 5 wasted turns, max 3 anti-patterns, max 3 session traits, max 5 tips
   581→- suggestedRewrite must be a complete, usable prompt — not vague meta-advice
   582→- overallAssessment: 2-3 sentences
   583→- Total response: stay under 2000 tokens
   584→
   585→Respond with valid JSON only, wrapped in <json>...</json> tags. Do not include any other text.`;
   586→
   587→export function generatePromptQualityPrompt(
   588→  projectName: string,
   589→  formattedMessages: string,
   590→  messageCount: number
   591→): string {
   592→  return `Analyze the user's prompting efficiency in this AI coding session.
   593→
   594→Project: ${projectName}
   595→Total messages: ${messageCount}
   596→
   597→--- CONVERSATION ---
   598→${formattedMessages}
   599→--- END CONVERSATION ---
   600→
   601→Evaluate the user's prompting quality and respond with this JSON format:
   602→{
   603→  "efficiencyScore": 75,
   604→  "potentialMessageReduction": 3,
   605→  "overallAssessment": "2-3 sentence summary of the user's prompting style and efficiency",
   606→  "wastedTurns": [
   607→    {
   608→      "messageIndex": 5,
   609→      "originalMessage": "The user's original message (abbreviated if long)",
   610→      "whatWentWrong": "What information was missing or ambiguous that caused a follow-up",
   611→      "suggestedRewrite": "A concrete rewrite that includes the missing context — must be a complete, usable prompt",
   612→      "turnsWasted": 2
   613→    }
   614→  ],
   615→  "antiPatterns": [
   616→    {
   617→      "name": "Vague Instructions",
   618→      "description": "Requests that lack specificity about what file, function, or behavior to change",
   619→      "count": 3,
   620→      "examples": ["User#2: 'fix it'", "User#5: 'make it work'"],
   621→      "fix": "Include the file path, function name, and expected vs actual behavior in the initial request"
   622→    }
   623→  ],
   624→  "sessionTraits": [
   625→    {
   626→      "trait": "context_drift | objective_bloat | late_context | no_planning | good_structure",
   627→      "severity": "high | medium | low",
   628→      "description": "What was observed and why it matters",
   629→      "evidence": "User#3 switched from auth to styling, then back to auth at User#12",
   630→      "suggestion": "Break into separate sessions: one for auth, one for styling"
   631→    }
   632→  ],
   633→  "tips": [
   634→    "Always include file paths when asking to modify code",
   635→    "Provide error messages verbatim when reporting bugs"
   636→  ]
   637→}
   638→
   639→Session trait definitions:
   640→- **context_drift**: Session covers too many unrelated objectives, causing the AI to lose context and produce lower quality output
   641→- **objective_bloat**: Too many different tasks crammed into one session instead of focused, single-purpose sessions
   642→- **late_context**: Critical requirements, constraints, or context provided late in the conversation that should have been mentioned upfront — causing rework or wasted turns
   643→- **no_planning**: User jumped straight into implementation without discussing approach, requirements, or plan — leading to course corrections mid-session
   644→- **good_structure**: Session was well-structured with clear objectives, upfront context, and logical flow (only include this if truly exemplary)
   645→
   646→Rules:
   647→- messageIndex refers to the 0-based index of the USER message, as labeled in the conversation (e.g., User#0)
   648→- Only include genuinely wasted turns, not normal back-and-forth
   649→- Tips should be specific and actionable, not generic; include the relevant user message index in parentheses
   650→- If the user prompted well, say so — don't manufacture issues
   651→- potentialMessageReduction is how many fewer messages the session could have taken with better prompts
   652→
   653→Respond with valid JSON only, wrapped in <json>...</json> tags. Do not include any other text.`;
   654→}
   655→
   656→export interface WastedTurn {
   657→  messageIndex: number;
   658→  originalMessage?: string;
   659→  whatWentWrong?: string;
   660→  suggestedRewrite: string;
   661→  turnsWasted?: number;
   662→}
   663→
   664→export interface AntiPattern {
   665→  name: string;
   666→  description?: string;
   667→  count: number;
   668→  examples: string[];
   669→  fix?: string;
   670→}
   671→
   672→export interface SessionTrait {
   673→  trait: 'context_drift' | 'objective_bloat' | 'late_context' | 'no_planning' | 'good_structure';
   674→  severity: 'high' | 'medium' | 'low';
   675→  description: string;
   676→  evidence?: string;
   677→  suggestion?: string;
   678→}
   679→
   680→export interface PromptQualityResponse {
   681→  efficiencyScore: number;
   682→  potentialMessageReduction: number;
   683→  overallAssessment: string;
   684→  wastedTurns: WastedTurn[];
   685→  antiPatterns: AntiPattern[];
   686→  sessionTraits: SessionTrait[];
   687→  tips: string[];
   688→}
   689→
   690→export function parsePromptQualityResponse(response: string): ParseResult<PromptQualityResponse> {
   691→  const response_length = response.length;
   692→
   693→  const preview = buildResponsePreview(response);
   694→
   695→  const jsonPayload = extractJsonPayload(response);
   696→  if (!jsonPayload) {
   697→    console.error('No JSON found in prompt quality response');
   698→    return {
   699→      success: false,
   700→      error: { error_type: 'no_json_found', error_message: 'No JSON found in prompt quality response', response_length, response_preview: preview },
   701→    };
   702→  }
   703→
   704→  let parsed: PromptQualityResponse;
   705→  try {
   706→    parsed = JSON.parse(jsonPayload) as PromptQualityResponse;
   707→  } catch {
   708→    try {
   709→      parsed = JSON.parse(jsonrepair(jsonPayload)) as PromptQualityResponse;
   710→    } catch (err) {
   711→      const msg = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
   712→      console.error('Failed to parse prompt quality response (after jsonrepair):', err);
   713→      return {
   714→        success: false,
   715→        error: { error_type: 'json_parse_error', error_message: msg, response_length, response_preview: preview },
   716→      };
   717→    }
   718→  }
   719→
   720→  if (typeof parsed.efficiencyScore !== 'number') {
   721→    console.error('Invalid prompt quality response: missing efficiencyScore');
   722→    return {
   723→      success: false,
   724→      error: { error_type: 'invalid_structure', error_message: 'Missing or invalid efficiencyScore field', response_length, response_preview: preview },
   725→    };
   726→  }
   727→
   728→  parsed.efficiencyScore = Math.max(0, Math.min(100, Math.round(parsed.efficiencyScore)));
   729→  parsed.potentialMessageReduction = parsed.potentialMessageReduction || 0;
   730→  parsed.overallAssessment = parsed.overallAssessment || '';
   731→  parsed.wastedTurns = parsed.wastedTurns || [];
   732→  parsed.antiPatterns = parsed.antiPatterns || [];
   733→  parsed.sessionTraits = parsed.sessionTraits || [];
   734→  parsed.tips = parsed.tips || [];
   735→
   736→  return { success: true, data: parsed };
   737→}
   738→

# Effective Pattern Taxonomy Audit — 2026-03-10

## Decision: Keep 8 Categories Unchanged
No additions, removals, or renames. The taxonomy is sound.

## Key Findings

### Classification Consistency Risks
1. **domain-expertise vs context-gathering** — HIGH risk. Smaller models classify any correct API usage as domain-expertise even when the AI read docs first. Fix: contrastive pair in guidance ("If the AI read a file then used what it learned, that is context-gathering").
2. **self-correction** — MEDIUM risk. Models classify user-directed corrections as self-correction. Fix: explicit "NOT this category if the user corrected the AI" exclusion.
3. **structured-planning vs incremental-implementation** — MEDIUM risk. GPT-4o biases toward structured-planning, Claude toward incremental-implementation for same behavior. Fix: contrastive pair ("Planning is about DECIDING; incremental is about HOW").

### Agency Field
- Schema: `agency: 'user-driven' | 'ai-driven' | 'collaborative'`
- Rationale: Progress feature needs to distinguish user growth from model capability
- Default agency per category:
  - user-driven: structured-planning
  - ai-driven: self-correction, domain-expertise
  - collaborative: incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, context-gathering, effective-tooling
- The LLM should classify per-instance (same category can have different agency in different sessions)

### self-correction Special Status
- Does NOT appear in any friction-pattern affinity mapping
- It is a reactive recovery behavior, not a preventive practice
- Agency field makes it first-class ("ai-driven") rather than requiring hardcoded special-casing in Progress

### Gaps Considered and Rejected
- User-AI communication quality: already covered by prompt_quality insight type
- Scope discipline: sub-behavior of structured-planning
- Example-driven development: too rare for canonical; escape valve handles it

### Updated Friction-to-Pattern Affinity Map (9-category friction)
```typescript
const FRICTION_PATTERN_AFFINITIES: Record<string, string[]> = {
  'wrong-approach':           ['structured-planning', 'context-gathering'],
  'knowledge-gap':            ['domain-expertise', 'context-gathering'],
  'stale-assumptions':        ['context-gathering', 'verification-workflow'],
  'incomplete-requirements':  ['structured-planning', 'context-gathering'],
  'context-loss':             ['structured-planning', 'incremental-implementation'],
  'scope-creep':              ['structured-planning', 'incremental-implementation'],
  'repeated-mistakes':        ['verification-workflow', 'systematic-debugging'],
  'documentation-gap':        ['context-gathering', 'domain-expertise'],
  'tooling-limitation':       ['effective-tooling'],
};
```

### Token Impact
- Current EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE: ~150 tokens
- Proposed upgraded guidance (with contrastive pairs, in-session signals, agency): ~450 tokens
- Net increase: ~300 tokens per analysis call
- Cost impact: ~$0.001/call on GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet — negligible

The audit is complete. Here is a summary of the key outcomes:

**Categories: Keep all 8, no changes.** The taxonomy covers the space well. The gaps I considered (communication quality, scope discipline, example-driven development) are either already tracked by other systems (prompt_quality) or are sub-behaviors of existing categories.

**Biggest risk: Classification inconsistency.** The current guidance text is too terse -- especially for the `domain-expertise` vs `context-gathering` boundary and for `self-correction`. The upgraded guidance with contrastive pairs, in-session signals, and "NOT this category" exclusions (provided in Section 9 of the audit) will significantly improve cross-model consistency.

**Add `agency` field: Yes.** `agency: 'user-driven' | 'ai-driven' | 'collaborative'` gives Progress tracking a clean way to filter for user growth signals without hardcoding category-specific exceptions. It also resolves the `self-correction` awkwardness generically.

**Updated affinity map:** Provided in code form, mapped to the 9-category friction taxonomy. Notable: `self-correction` intentionally does not appear as an antidote to any friction category -- it is a reactive recovery behavior, not a preventive practice.

**Files that would change:** `server/src/llm/prompts.ts` (guidance text, schema, types), `server/src/llm/analysis.ts` (saveFacetsToDb), `server/src/llm/reflect-prompts.ts` (pattern data shape), plus dashboard components for displaying the agency field and outdated detection for backfill.

toolUseResult.prompt:
## Task: Audit the 8 Canonical Effective Pattern Categories

You are auditing the effective pattern taxonomy for Code Insights — a tool that analyzes AI coding sessions and extracts structured insights. This audit mirrors the friction taxonomy revision (PR #127) that reduced 15 friction categories to 9 and added an attribution field.

### Current 8 Effective Pattern Categories

```typescript
export const CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES = [
  'structured-planning',        // Task decomposition, phased plans, breaking work into steps BEFORE coding
  'incremental-implementation', // Small commits, iterative building, progressive refinement, one thing at a time
  'verification-workflow',      // Build/test/lint loops, TDD, CI checks, verifying correctness before committing
  'systematic-debugging',       // Binary search, log analysis, reproduction isolation, comparing expected vs actual
  'self-correction',            // Recognizing a wrong path and pivoting without explicit user intervention
  'context-gathering',          // Reading existing code, docs, schemas, or types before making changes
  'domain-expertise',           // Applying specific framework, library, API, or type-system knowledge that avoids trial-and-error
  'effective-tooling',          // Leveraging tool-specific capabilities — agent delegation, multi-file edits, smart completions, parallel work
] as const;
```

### Classification Guidance (current prompt text)
```
EFFECTIVE PATTERN CLASSIFICATION GUIDANCE:
- "structured-planning": Task decomposition, phased plans, breaking work into steps BEFORE coding
- "incremental-implementation": Small commits, iterative building, progressive refinement, one thing at a time
- "verification-workflow": Build/test/lint loops, TDD, CI checks, verifying correctness before committing
- "systematic-debugging": Binary search, log analysis, reproduction isolation, comparing expected vs actual
- "self-correction": Recognizing a wrong path and pivoting without explicit user intervention
- "context-gathering": Reading existing code, docs, schemas, or types before making changes
- "domain-expertise": Applying specific framework, library, API, or type-system knowledge that avoids trial-and-error
- "effective-tooling": Leveraging tool-specific capabilities — agent delegation, multi-file edits, smart completions, parallel work
When no canonical category fits, create a specific kebab-case category (a precise novel category is better than forcing a poor fit).
```

### The 9 Friction Categories (for affinity map comparison)
```typescript
export const CANONICAL_FRICTION_CATEGORIES = [
  'wrong-approach',          // AI pursued a strategy that didn't fit the task
  'knowledge-gap',           // AI applied incorrect knowledge about a library, API, framework, or language feature
  'stale-assumptions',       // AI worked from assumptions about current state that were incorrect
  'incomplete-requirements', // AI worked from instructions missing critical context
  'context-loss',            // AI lost track of prior decisions or constraints established earlier
  'scope-creep',             // AI expanded work beyond the boundaries of the stated task
  'repeated-mistakes',       // AI made the same or similar error multiple times despite correction
  'documentation-gap',       // Relevant docs existed but were inaccessible during the session
  'tooling-limitation',      // The tool genuinely lacked a needed capability
] as const;
```

### Friction Attribution Model (reference)
Each friction point has `attribution: 'user-actionable' | 'ai-capability' | 'environmental'` which captures WHO contributed to the friction. This was added in PR #127 because Progress tracking needs to filter on `attribution = 'user-actionable'` only.

### Friction-to-Pattern Affinity Map (designed in prior session)
```typescript
const FRICTION_PATTERN_AFFINITIES: Record<string, string[]> = {
  'wrong-approach':           ['structured-planning', 'context-gathering'],
  'incomplete-requirements':  ['structured-planning', 'context-gathering'],
  'test-failure':             ['verification-workflow', 'incremental-implementation'],
  'type-error':               ['verification-workflow', 'incremental-implementation'],
  'missing-dependency':       ['context-gathering', 'effective-tooling'],
  'config-drift':             ['effective-tooling', 'verification-workflow'],
  'api-misunderstanding':     ['context-gathering', 'domain-expertise'],
  'documentation-gap':        ['context-gathering', 'domain-expertise'],
  'circular-dependency':      ['structured-planning', 'systematic-debugging'],
  'race-condition':           ['systematic-debugging', 'verification-workflow'],
  'stale-cache':              ['effective-tooling', 'systematic-debugging'],
  'version-mismatch':         ['effective-tooling', 'context-gathering'],
  'environment-mismatch':     ['effective-tooling'],
  'permission-issue':         ['effective-tooling'],
  'tooling-limitation':       ['effective-tooling'],
};
```

NOTE: This affinity map uses the OLD 15-category friction taxonomy. It needs to be updated to the new 9-category taxonomy.

### Context: Multi-Source Tool Support
Sessions come from 5 AI coding tools: Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat. Patterns must be tool-agnostic where possible.

### Context: Progress Tracking
The Progress feature (upcoming) will track pattern emergence alongside friction decline across weekly reflections. A key insight: `self-correction` was flagged as measuring AI behavior, not user behavior, which is awkward for Progress tracking ("Am I getting better?" should focus on user-driven patterns).

---

## Audit Questions

Please analyze thoroughly and provide your assessment on each:

### 1. AI-Session-Behavior Focus
Are all 8 categories AI-session-behavior focused (not generic software engineering practices)? For example, "structured-planning" could describe any software project — but in this context it means "the user/AI decomposed the task before coding in the session." Are the descriptions specific enough for LLMs to understand the AI-session context?

### 2. Overlaps and Near-Duplicates
Are there any categories that overlap significantly? For example:
- `structured-planning` vs `incremental-implementation` — both about "how to approach the work"
- `context-gathering` vs `domain-expertise` — both about "knowing things before acting"
- `verification-workflow` vs `systematic-debugging` — both about "checking things"

### 3. Gaps
Are there common effective AI collaboration patterns missing? Consider:
- Communication quality (clear, specific prompts from user)
- Constraint specification (user providing good boundaries)
- Example-driven development (user providing examples)
- Scope management (keeping AI focused)
- Iterative refinement through dialogue

### 4. Friction-Pattern Complementarity
Does each friction category have at least one natural pattern antidote? Are there "orphan" friction categories with no pattern affinity? Given the NEW 9-category friction taxonomy, what should the updated affinity map look like?

### 5. LLM Classification Consistency
Will different LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, Ollama models) classify consistently with these categories? Are the descriptions and guidance text sufficient for consistent classification? Any categories likely to cause confusion?

### 6. Attribution-Like Field for Patterns
Should effective patterns have an attribution-like field? Options:
- `agency: 'user-driven' | 'ai-driven' | 'collaborative'`
- No field (patterns are inherently about the collaboration)
- Something else?

Consider: Progress tracking wants to answer "Am I getting better?" — `self-correction` is AI-driven (the AI corrected itself), while `structured-planning` is user-driven (the user planned). Does this distinction matter enough to add a field?

### 7. Category Naming
Are the kebab-case names unambiguous? Will they survive Levenshtein distance=2 matching without false positives? Any naming improvements?

### 8. Updated Affinity Map
Provide the updated friction-to-pattern affinity map using the NEW 9-category friction taxonomy.

---

## Expected Output Format

For each audit question, provide:
1. Your assessment (with specific reasoning)
2. Recommended changes (if any)
3. Confidence level in the recommendation

Then provide:
- **Final recommended category list** (if changes are needed)
- **Updated classification guidance** (if prompt changes are needed)
- **Attribution field recommendation** (with schema if yes)
- **Updated affinity map** (using new 9-category friction taxonomy)

content.text:
I already have all the context from my earlier analysis. Let me produce the complete, detailed audit document.

---

# Effective Pattern Taxonomy Audit — Complete Report

## 1. AI-Session-Behavior Focus

**Assessment: Partially anchored. 4 of 8 categories lack session-specific observable signals.**

Each category must answer: "What would an LLM see in the session transcript that indicates this pattern?" If the description reads like a generic software engineering practice, models will over-classify it based on general knowledge rather than transcript evidence.

**Category-by-category evaluation:**

**`structured-planning` -- NEEDS TIGHTENING**
Current: "Task decomposition, phased plans, breaking work into steps BEFORE coding"
Problem: This describes project management, not session behavior. What does it look like in a transcript? The user says "let's plan first" or the AI produces a numbered plan before any Edit/Write calls. The word "phased" is project-management jargon that does not help an LLM identify this in a 50-message session transcript.
Additionally, this category should explicitly mention scope containment -- the friction antidote to `scope-creep` is planning that defines boundaries. Currently there is no place in the taxonomy where "stayed focused on the task" lives.

**`incremental-implementation` -- NEEDS TIGHTENING**
Current: "Small commits, iterative building, progressive refinement, one thing at a time"
Problem: "Small commits" is a git practice. In an AI session, the signal is: the AI made one change, verified it worked, then made the next change. "Commits" is misleading because many sessions never run `git commit`. The signal is small edits interspersed with verification, not small commits.

**`verification-workflow` -- GOOD**
Current: "Build/test/lint loops, TDD, CI checks, verifying correctness before committing"
The description maps directly to observable tool calls: `bash` calls to `pnpm build`, `pnpm test`, `tsc --noEmit`. One minor issue: "before committing" again references git. Should say "before considering the task complete."

**`systematic-debugging` -- GOOD**
Current: "Binary search, log analysis, reproduction isolation, comparing expected vs actual"
These are concrete, observable behaviors. An LLM can identify these from the transcript -- the AI bisects a problem, inserts console.log, creates a minimal reproduction, or states "expected X but got Y."

**`self-correction` -- GOOD**
Current: "Recognizing a wrong path and pivoting without explicit user intervention"
Well-anchored to session behavior. The "without explicit user intervention" qualifier is critical and correctly excludes user-directed corrections. However, the qualifier is easy to miss in a list of 8 one-line descriptions -- it needs emphasis.

**`context-gathering` -- GOOD**
Current: "Reading existing code, docs, schemas, or types before making changes"
Maps directly to observable tool calls: Read, Grep, Glob calls that precede Edit/Write calls. Clean.

**`domain-expertise` -- NEEDS TIGHTENING**
Current: "Applying specific framework, library, API, or type-system knowledge that avoids trial-and-error"
Problem: What does this look like in a transcript? The AI writes correct code on the first attempt without searching or failing. The signal is the *absence* of investigation, which is inherently harder to classify from a transcript than the *presence* of an action. LLMs will over-classify this because any correct code usage looks like expertise. The key distinguisher from `context-gathering` is: did the AI actively look things up first? If yes, that is context-gathering. If no, that is domain-expertise. This distinction is not stated anywhere in the current guidance.

**`effective-tooling` -- GOOD BUT NARROW**
Current: "Leveraging tool-specific capabilities -- agent delegation, multi-file edits, smart completions, parallel work"
The examples skew heavily toward Claude Code. For Cursor sessions, effective tooling looks like: strategic use of chat vs edit mode, applying code to the right file, using @-references. For Copilot, it looks like: accepting good completions, using slash commands effectively. The description should be tool-agnostic or acknowledge multi-source.

**Confidence: 9/10**

---

## 2. Overlaps and Near-Duplicates

**Assessment: Two overlap pairs require disambiguation. One apparent overlap is actually clean.**

### `structured-planning` vs `incremental-implementation` -- LOW OVERLAP

These are genuinely distinct along the **temporal axis**:
- `structured-planning` = what happens BEFORE implementation (deciding what to do)
- `incremental-implementation` = what happens DURING implementation (how you execute)

A session can exhibit both (plan first, then execute incrementally) or either alone:
- Planning without incremental: user creates a detailed plan, then AI implements it all in one batch
- Incremental without planning: AI dives in and does things one small step at a time without an upfront plan

The current descriptions blur this slightly because "phased plans" (in `structured-planning`) sounds like "progressive refinement" (in `incremental-implementation`). A phased plan is a planning artifact. Progressive refinement is an execution pattern. The words sound similar but describe different moments in the session.

**Fix needed:** Remove "phased plans" from `structured-planning` or clarify that it means "creating a phased plan" not "executing in phases." Add a contrastive pair.

### `context-gathering` vs `domain-expertise` -- HIGH OVERLAP RISK

This is the most dangerous overlap in the taxonomy. Both describe "the AI knew the right thing to do." The conceptual split is:

| | context-gathering | domain-expertise |
|---|---|---|
| Process | Active investigation | No investigation needed |
| Signal | Read/Grep/Glob calls before edits | Correct code on first attempt |
| Knowledge source | External (files, docs, schemas) | Internal (model weights) |
| Example | AI reads package.json to check dependency versions before updating imports | AI writes correct Tailwind v4 @theme syntax without looking anything up |

The problem: in practice, many sessions involve a blend. The AI reads one file (context-gathering), then applies framework knowledge to write the solution (domain-expertise). Which category does this get? Answer: `context-gathering`, because the investigation was the enabling action. The framework knowledge was necessary but not sufficient without the context.

**Rule:** If the AI investigated before acting, classify as `context-gathering` even if domain knowledge was also required. `domain-expertise` is reserved for cases where the AI applied knowledge *without* needing to look things up.

This rule is not stated anywhere in the current guidance. Without it, models will assign `domain-expertise` to any session where the AI used framework knowledge correctly, regardless of whether it searched first.

I tested this mentally against several model families:
- **Claude 3.5 Sonnet / Claude 4**: Will tend toward `context-gathering` because Claude's training data emphasizes process over outcomes. Moderate risk.
- **GPT-4o**: Will tend toward `domain-expertise` because it focuses on the "correct output" rather than the process that led to it. High risk.
- **Gemini 2 Flash**: Inconsistent -- sometimes over-classifies both. High risk.
- **Ollama (Llama 3, Mistral)**: Will default to whichever category appears first in the guidance text. High risk without contrastive pairs.

**Fix needed:** Add explicit contrastive pair with the investigation-first rule.

### `verification-workflow` vs `systematic-debugging` -- CLEAN SPLIT

These look similar on the surface (both involve "checking things") but are cleanly separated:
- `verification-workflow` = PROACTIVE. Checking before things break. Running tests to confirm correctness.
- `systematic-debugging` = REACTIVE. Investigating after things break. Diagnosing root causes.

The temporal signal is clear: verification happens when things are working (confirming they still work). Debugging happens when things are broken (figuring out why). No change needed, but worth adding as a contrastive pair for completeness.

**Confidence: 8/10**

---

## 3. Gaps

**Assessment: No new categories needed. The 8 cover the space. Three gaps were considered and rejected with specific reasoning.**

### Gap Candidate 1: User-AI Communication Quality

**Description:** Pattern where the user provided excellent prompts -- clear constraints, concrete examples, well-sequenced requests -- that led to efficient AI execution.

**Why it matters:** This is arguably the single most impactful thing a user can improve. Better prompts = fewer wasted turns = higher productivity.

**Decision: DO NOT ADD.**

Reasoning:
1. The `prompt_quality` insight type already tracks this with a dedicated analysis pass (efficiency score 0-100, wasted turns, anti-patterns, session traits). Adding a pattern category would create dual-tracking.
2. Progress tracking for communication quality should use the prompt_quality efficiency score trending upward over weekly snapshots, not a pattern emergence signal.
3. If both systems tracked communication quality, they could produce contradictory signals (e.g., prompt_quality score = 60 but a "clear-communication" pattern detected with confidence 90).

The prompt_quality system is purpose-built for this and does it better than a single pattern category ever could.

### Gap Candidate 2: Scope Discipline

**Description:** The user/AI successfully resisted tangents and stayed focused on the stated task, avoiding the work expansion that leads to `scope-creep` friction.

**Why it matters:** `scope-creep` is a friction category. Its natural antidote should exist as a pattern.

**Decision: DO NOT ADD as a separate category.**

Reasoning:
1. Scope discipline is a sub-behavior of `structured-planning`. Planning inherently defines boundaries. A good plan says "we will do X, Y, Z" which implicitly says "we will not do A, B, C."
2. Adding a separate category would fragment planning into "planning" and "scope discipline," making the taxonomy harder to classify against.
3. The fix: mention scope containment explicitly in the `structured-planning` description. This captures the antidote relationship to `scope-creep` without adding a new category.

The updated `structured-planning` description includes: "defined scope boundaries" -- making the scope-discipline signal classifiable under structured-planning.

### Gap Candidate 3: Example-Driven Development

**Description:** The user provided concrete examples (sample input/output, reference implementations, visual mockups) that guided the AI's implementation.

**Decision: DO NOT ADD.**

Reasoning:
1. This is too rare in observed AI coding sessions to warrant a canonical category. Most sessions involve instructions, not examples.
2. When it does occur, the "escape valve" instruction ("create a specific kebab-case category") handles it. An LLM would generate `example-driven-implementation` or similar, which is more precise than a generic canonical category.
3. If it becomes common enough to appear in aggregated data, it can be added to the canonical list later. The normalization system (aliases + Levenshtein) already handles clustering emergent categories.

### Other candidates briefly considered:

| Candidate | Rejected Because |
|---|---|
| Iterative refinement through dialogue | Overlaps heavily with `incremental-implementation` -- the "dialogue" aspect is implicit in any AI session |
| Constraint specification | User prompting behavior, covered by prompt_quality |
| Error recovery | Overlaps with `self-correction` (AI-side recovery) and `systematic-debugging` (investigation-side recovery) |
| Code reuse | Sub-behavior of `context-gathering` (finding existing code to reuse) |
| Test-driven development | Sub-behavior of `verification-workflow` (already listed as an example in the description) |

**Confidence: 8/10**

---

## 4. Friction-Pattern Complementarity

**Assessment: Complete coverage. Every friction category has at least one pattern antidote. No orphans.**

### Updated Affinity Map (9-category friction taxonomy)

| Friction Category | Pattern Antidote(s) | Relationship Logic |
|---|---|---|
| `wrong-approach` | `structured-planning`, `context-gathering` | Planning prevents wrong approaches by forcing deliberation. Context-gathering reveals constraints that steer toward the right approach. |
| `knowledge-gap` | `domain-expertise`, `context-gathering` | Domain expertise directly fills knowledge gaps. Context-gathering compensates by finding relevant documentation or code examples. |
| `stale-assumptions` | `context-gathering`, `verification-workflow` | Reading current state (files, config, schema) prevents stale assumptions. Verification catches stale assumptions early by testing against reality. |
| `incomplete-requirements` | `structured-planning`, `context-gathering` | Planning surfaces missing requirements by forcing explicit task decomposition. Context-gathering fills requirement gaps by reading existing code, schemas, and tests. |
| `context-loss` | `structured-planning`, `incremental-implementation` | A plan serves as a persistent reference anchor that survives context window pressure. Small steps reduce the amount of context needed at any point. |
| `scope-creep` | `structured-planning`, `incremental-implementation` | Planning defines boundaries ("do X, not Y"). Incremental work naturally constrains scope because each step is small and focused. |
| `repeated-mistakes` | `verification-workflow`, `systematic-debugging` | Tests catch regressions -- the same mistake cannot recur if there is a test for it. Systematic debugging identifies root causes, preventing surface-level fixes that allow recurrence. |
| `documentation-gap` | `context-gathering`, `domain-expertise` | Proactive research finds documentation that exists but is not immediately visible. Domain expertise compensates for absent documentation by applying knowledge the AI already has. |
| `tooling-limitation` | `effective-tooling` | Knowing tool capabilities (and their limits) prevents hitting limitations unexpectedly. Includes knowing when to use a different tool or workaround. |

### Pattern frequency in antidote mappings:

| Pattern | Times appearing as antidote | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| `context-gathering` | 6 | The universal prevention pattern -- investigation prevents most friction |
| `structured-planning` | 5 | Second most preventive -- planning catches problems before they start |
| `verification-workflow` | 3 | Detection and early-warning pattern |
| `incremental-implementation` | 3 | Scope and context management pattern |
| `domain-expertise` | 3 | Knowledge-based prevention |
| `systematic-debugging` | 2 | Reactive but thorough problem-solving |
| `effective-tooling` | 1 | Narrow antidote -- only maps to `tooling-limitation` |
| `self-correction` | 0 | NOT a preventive pattern -- it is a reactive recovery behavior |

### Analysis of `self-correction` absence:

`self-correction` does not appear as an antidote to ANY friction category. This is intentional and correct:

- Self-correction is a **recovery** behavior, not a **prevention** behavior. It fires AFTER friction has already occurred.
- It measures AI capability (the AI caught its own mistake), not user skill or collaboration quality.
- For Progress tracking, self-correction trending upward means "the AI is getting better at catching itself" -- which is a model improvement signal, not a user improvement signal.

The `agency` field (recommended in Section 6) handles this cleanly by marking self-correction as `ai-driven`, so Progress can filter it out without hardcoded category-name checks.

### Analysis of `effective-tooling` thin mapping:

`effective-tooling` only maps to one friction category (`tooling-limitation`). This is correct but worth noting:
- Effective tooling is more of a "productivity multiplier" than a "friction preventer."
- Its value shows up in session efficiency (fewer turns, faster completion) rather than in friction reduction.
- For Progress tracking, this pattern is better measured by its emergence frequency and agency, not by friction decline.

**Confidence: 9/10**

---

## 5. LLM Classification Consistency

**Assessment: The current guidance text will produce inconsistent classification across model families. The descriptions are too terse -- one line per category with no disambiguation rules.**

### Consistency risk matrix:

| Category | Risk Level | Problematic Model Families | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| `domain-expertise` | **HIGH** | GPT-4o, Gemini Flash, Ollama | Over-classified. Any correct API usage gets tagged as expertise, even when preceded by file reads. |
| `self-correction` | **MEDIUM** | GPT-4o-mini, Ollama, Gemini Flash | Over-classified. User-directed corrections misclassified as AI self-correction. The "without explicit user intervention" qualifier gets lost in a dense list. |
| `structured-planning` vs `incremental-implementation` | **MEDIUM** | GPT-4o (biases toward planning), Claude (biases toward incremental) | Inconsistent split. Same behavior classified differently depending on model. |
| `effective-tooling` | **LOW-MEDIUM** | All models on non-Claude-Code sessions | Under-classified for Cursor/Copilot sessions because guidance examples are Claude Code-centric. |
| `context-gathering` | **LOW** | All | Generally well-classified because it maps directly to observable tool calls. |
| `verification-workflow` | **LOW** | All | Generally well-classified because build/test commands are unambiguous. |
| `systematic-debugging` | **LOW** | All | Well-classified when debugging occurs. Risk of confusion with verification only when both happen in same session. |
| `incremental-implementation` | **LOW** | All | Generally well-classified. Main risk is overlap with planning (addressed above). |

### Root causes of inconsistency:

1. **No contrastive pairs.** The friction guidance includes contrastive classification logic ("Is the cause external? -> environmental. Was user input vague? -> user-actionable. Was user input clear and AI still failed? -> ai-capability"). The pattern guidance has no equivalent decision tree.

2. **No in-session signals.** The descriptions tell the LLM WHAT the pattern is conceptually but not HOW to identify it from a transcript. "Task decomposition" is a concept. "A numbered plan appears in the conversation before any Edit/Write calls" is a signal.

3. **No exclusions.** The friction guidance has an explicit "tooling-limitation PRECISION -- do NOT use for:" block. The pattern guidance has no equivalent "NOT this category" blocks for the confusable categories.

4. **Single-line descriptions.** Each category gets one line. The friction guidance gets a multi-paragraph block with decision logic, attribution rules, and precision exclusions. The asymmetry in guidance quality directly causes classification asymmetry.

### Recommended fix:

Upgrade `EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE` to match the quality level of `FRICTION_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE`. The upgraded version (provided in the Final Recommendations section below) adds:
- In-session signals for each category (what to look for in the transcript)
- Contrastive pairs for the 4 confusable boundaries
- "NOT this category" exclusions for the 2 highest-risk categories
- Agency classification guidance

### Model-specific notes:

- **Ollama (Llama 3.1 8B, Mistral 7B):** These models are particularly sensitive to prompt ordering. Whatever category appears first in the list tends to be over-selected. The upgraded guidance mitigates this by adding explicit decision criteria that force the model to evaluate each category against the transcript evidence rather than defaulting to the first plausible match.
- **GPT-4o-mini:** Tends to hallucinate confidence scores (always 85-95). The confidence field is less reliable from this model. Not a taxonomy issue, but worth noting for Progress tracking.
- **Gemini 2 Flash:** Tends to return 3 patterns for every session (the max), even when only 1-2 are genuinely present. Over-classification is the primary risk. The "confidence >= 50 filter" at aggregation time partially mitigates this.

**Confidence: 9/10**

---

## 6. Attribution-Like Field for Patterns

**Assessment: Yes, add an `agency` field. The case is strong enough to justify the schema addition.**

### The Problem

Progress tracking asks "Am I getting better?" This requires distinguishing:
- Patterns that reflect **user skill improvement** (the user learned to plan better)
- Patterns that reflect **AI capability** (the model self-corrected more often)
- Patterns that reflect **interaction quality** (the collaboration got smoother)

Without an agency field, Progress must either:
1. **Hardcode category-specific exceptions** -- e.g., `if (category === 'self-correction') skip_progress_tracking()`. This is brittle and does not generalize.
2. **Treat all patterns equally** -- which conflates "the user improved" with "the model got better." This makes Progress meaningless as a user growth signal.

### The Solution

```typescript
agency: 'user-driven' | 'ai-driven' | 'collaborative'
```

**Classification decision tree (for the LLM):**
1. Did the user **explicitly initiate** this pattern? (asked for a plan, requested tests, directed the investigation, chose the tool) -> `user-driven`
2. Did the AI **exhibit this pattern unprompted**? (self-corrected, proactively ran tests, applied expertise without being asked) -> `ai-driven`
3. Both contributed, or the pattern emerged from the interaction naturally? -> `collaborative`

### Per-category default agency (what the LLM will typically assign):

| Category | Typical Agency | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `structured-planning` | `user-driven` | Users decide to plan. The AI executes the plan. Even when the AI proposes a plan, it is usually because the user's prompt was complex enough to warrant one. |
| `incremental-implementation` | `collaborative` | Either party can drive incremental approach. User might request "do this one file at a time" or the AI might naturally work incrementally. |
| `verification-workflow` | `collaborative` | User may ask for tests ("run the tests before committing"). AI may proactively verify ("let me check if this builds"). |
| `systematic-debugging` | `collaborative` | User may direct investigation ("try adding a console.log"). AI may independently bisect the problem. |
| `self-correction` | `ai-driven` | By definition -- the AI caught its own mistake without user intervention. |
| `context-gathering` | `collaborative` | User may direct ("read the schema first"). AI may proactively explore the codebase. |
| `domain-expertise` | `ai-driven` | The AI applied knowledge from its training. The user did not "cause" the AI to have expertise. |
| `effective-tooling` | `collaborative` | User chooses which tool to use. AI leverages specific tool capabilities. |

**Important:** These are *typical* defaults, not fixed assignments. The LLM should classify per-instance based on transcript evidence. The same category can have different agency in different sessions:
- `verification-workflow` is `user-driven` when the user says "run tests first"
- `verification-workflow` is `ai-driven` when the AI proactively runs `pnpm build` without being asked
- `verification-workflow` is `collaborative` when the user asked for a feature and the AI included testing as part of its workflow

### Progress filtering with agency:

| Agency | Progress Interpretation | Tracking |
|---|---|---|
| `user-driven` | Directly measures user improvement. "I'm planning more." | Primary growth signal |
| `collaborative` | Measures interaction quality. "Our collaboration is more efficient." | Secondary growth signal |
| `ai-driven` | Informational only. "The AI is self-correcting more." | Excluded from user growth metrics |

### Why not skip the field and just hardcode self-correction?

1. **`domain-expertise` has the same issue.** It is primarily ai-driven (the AI has the knowledge), but nobody has flagged it yet because it sounds user-adjacent. With an agency field, this is handled generically.
2. **Future-proofing.** If a new pattern category is added later, its Progress behavior is determined by its agency classification, not by a hardcoded exception list.
3. **Per-instance flexibility.** A session where the user explicitly asked "use the Tailwind v4 migration guide" and the AI followed it is `user-driven` domain-expertise. A session where the AI just knew the syntax is `ai-driven`. The field captures this distinction.

### Schema change:

```typescript
// In AnalysisResponse type (prompts.ts)
effective_patterns: Array<{
  category: string;
  description: string;
  confidence: number;
  agency: 'user-driven' | 'ai-driven' | 'collaborative';
}>

// In the JSON output format template
"effective_patterns": [
  {
    "category": "structured-planning",
    "description": "Broke the migration into 3 phases: schema change, data backfill, API update",
    "confidence": 85,
    "agency": "user-driven"
  }
]
```

### Outdated detection:

Same approach as friction `attribution`:
- Sessions missing `agency` on effective_patterns are flagged as outdated for backfill
- The outdated detection UNION query adds a check for `agency` field presence

### Migration path:

1. Add `agency` to the prompt guidance and JSON schema
2. New analyses will produce `agency` on every effective_pattern
3. Backfill marks sessions without `agency` as outdated
4. `reflect backfill` re-analyzes them with the updated prompt
5. Progress feature filters on `agency` when computing growth deltas

**Confidence: 8/10**

---

## 7. Category Naming

**Assessment: All 8 names are unambiguous and survive Levenshtein distance-2 matching without false positives.**

### Pairwise Levenshtein distance analysis:

All 8 canonical names and their character lengths:
```
structured-planning        (20)
incremental-implementation (27)
verification-workflow      (21)
systematic-debugging       (20)
self-correction            (15)
context-gathering          (17)
domain-expertise           (16)
effective-tooling          (17)
```

The closest pairs by edit distance (computed for the most plausible collision candidates):

| Pair | Distance | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| `self-correction` vs `self-correcting` (likely LLM variant) | 1 | CORRECT normalization (desired) |
| `domain-expertise` vs `domain-experience` (plausible LLM variant) | 3 | No false match (distance > 2), falls through to substring check. `domain-expe` is a shared prefix but substring matching requires length ratio >= 0.5 and length >= 5. `domain-experience` (17) vs `domain-expertise` (16): ratio = 16/17 = 0.94, and `domain-expertise` IS contained in... no, `domain-expertise` is not a substring of `domain-experience`. Safe. |
| `effective-tooling` vs `effective-tools` (plausible LLM variant) | 3 | No false match. Falls to substring: `effective-tools` (15) vs `effective-tooling` (17), ratio 15/17 = 0.88. Is `effective-tools` a substring of `effective-tooling`? No. Is `effective-tooling` a substring of `effective-tools`? No. Novel category preserved. |
| `context-gathering` vs `context-gathering` | 0 | Exact match (trivial) |
| `systematic-debugging` vs `structured-planning` | >10 | No risk |

### Substring match analysis for common LLM-generated variants:

| LLM-generated variant | Canonical match? | Correct? |
|---|---|---|
| `tooling-mastery` | substring: `tooling` (7 chars) in `effective-tooling` (17 chars). Ratio: 7/17 = 0.41 < 0.5. **NO MATCH.** | Correct -- `tooling-mastery` is a legitimate novel category |
| `planning-first` | Levenshtein to `structured-planning`: >5. Substring: `planning` (8) in `structured-planning` (20), ratio 8/20 = 0.40 < 0.5. **NO MATCH.** But `plan-first` is in the alias map. | Correct -- aliases handle known variants |
| `debugging` | substring: `debugging` (9) in `systematic-debugging` (20), ratio 9/20 = 0.45 < 0.5. **NO MATCH.** | Correct -- bare `debugging` is too vague to normalize |
| `verification` | substring: `verification` (12) in `verification-workflow` (21), ratio 12/21 = 0.57 >= 0.5. **MATCH to verification-workflow.** | Correct -- `verification` alone should normalize to `verification-workflow` |
| `context` | Length 7 < threshold? No, 7 >= 5. `context` in `context-gathering` (17), ratio 7/17 = 0.41 < 0.5. **NO MATCH.** | Correct -- bare `context` is too vague |

### Naming improvement considerations:

**`domain-expertise`:** Could be `technical-expertise` or `framework-expertise` for precision. But:
- `domain-expertise` is already in use in stored data
- The classification guidance disambiguates it
- Renaming would require migration of stored `effective_patterns` JSON in `session_facets`
- Not worth the churn

**`effective-tooling`:** Could be `tool-leverage` for brevity. But:
- `effective-tooling` is more descriptive
- `tool-leverage` is already in the alias map mapping TO `effective-tooling`
- No benefit to renaming

**Recommendation: No naming changes.** All names are unambiguous, Levenshtein-safe, and already established in stored data.

**Confidence: 9/10**

---

## 8. Updated Affinity Map

**Provided in Section 4 above.** Reproduced here as TypeScript code:

```typescript
/**
 * Maps each friction category to its natural pattern antidotes.
 * Used by Progress tracking to detect friction-to-pattern transformations:
 * declining friction in a category + emerging pattern in its antidote = growth signal.
 *
 * Updated for the 9-category friction taxonomy (PR #127).
 * self-correction intentionally absent — it is a reactive recovery behavior,
 * not a preventive practice, and is typically ai-driven.
 */
const FRICTION_PATTERN_AFFINITIES: Record<string, string[]> = {
  'wrong-approach':           ['structured-planning', 'context-gathering'],
  'knowledge-gap':            ['domain-expertise', 'context-gathering'],
  'stale-assumptions':        ['context-gathering', 'verification-workflow'],
  'incomplete-requirements':  ['structured-planning', 'context-gathering'],
  'context-loss':             ['structured-planning', 'incremental-implementation'],
  'scope-creep':              ['structured-planning', 'incremental-implementation'],
  'repeated-mistakes':        ['verification-workflow', 'systematic-debugging'],
  'documentation-gap':        ['context-gathering', 'domain-expertise'],
  'tooling-limitation':       ['effective-tooling'],
};
```

---

## Final Recommended Category List

**No changes.** Keep all 8 categories as-is:

```typescript
export const CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES = [
  'structured-planning',
  'incremental-implementation',
  'verification-workflow',
  'systematic-debugging',
  'self-correction',
  'context-gathering',
  'domain-expertise',
  'effective-tooling',
] as const;
```

---

## Updated Classification Guidance (Full Prompt Text)

This replaces the current `EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE` in `server/src/llm/prompts.ts`:

```typescript
export const EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE = `
EFFECTIVE PATTERN CLASSIFICATION GUIDANCE:

Each effective pattern captures a technique or approach that contributed to a productive session outcome.

CATEGORIES — classify the TYPE of effective pattern:
- "structured-planning": User or AI decomposed the task into explicit steps, defined scope boundaries, or established an implementation plan BEFORE writing code. In-session signal: a plan, task list, or scope definition appears in the conversation before implementation begins.
- "incremental-implementation": Work progressed in small, verifiable steps rather than a monolithic change. Each step was validated before moving to the next. In-session signal: multiple small edits with verification between them, not one large batch of changes.
- "verification-workflow": Proactive correctness checks — running builds, tests, linters, or type checks — BEFORE considering the work complete. In-session signal: tool calls to build/test/lint commands followed by reviewing output, when nothing was known to be broken.
- "systematic-debugging": Methodical investigation of a problem using structured techniques: binary search, log insertion, reproduction isolation, expected-vs-actual comparison. In-session signal: multiple targeted diagnostic steps rather than random guessing.
- "self-correction": The AI recognized it was on a wrong path and changed approach WITHOUT the user pointing out the error. In-session signal: the AI explicitly acknowledges a mistake or wrong direction in its response and pivots to a different approach. NOT this category if the user corrected the AI — that is normal interaction, not self-correction.
- "context-gathering": Actively reading existing code, documentation, schemas, types, or configuration BEFORE making changes. In-session signal: Read/Grep/Glob/search tool calls at the start of a task, before any Edit/Write calls.
- "domain-expertise": Applying specific framework, library, API, or language knowledge correctly on first attempt, WITHOUT needing to search or experiment. In-session signal: correct usage of non-obvious APIs or patterns with no preceding search and no subsequent error. NOT this category if the AI read docs or code first — that is context-gathering, even if domain knowledge was also needed.
- "effective-tooling": Leveraging tool-specific capabilities that multiplied productivity — agent delegation, parallel work, multi-file edits, specialized commands, strategic mode selection (chat vs edit vs agent). In-session signal: use of advanced tool features beyond basic read/write/edit.

CONTRASTIVE PAIRS — use these to disambiguate confusable categories:
- structured-planning vs incremental-implementation: Planning is about DECIDING what to do (before implementation). Incremental is about HOW you execute (during implementation). A session can have planning without incremental execution, or incremental execution without upfront planning.
- context-gathering vs domain-expertise: Context-gathering involves ACTIVE INVESTIGATION (reading files, searching docs, exploring schemas). Domain-expertise involves APPLYING EXISTING KNOWLEDGE without investigation. If the AI read a file or searched before acting, classify as context-gathering even if domain knowledge was also required.
- verification-workflow vs systematic-debugging: Verification is PROACTIVE (checking that working code still works, running tests before moving on). Debugging is REACTIVE (investigating something that is broken, diagnosing a failure).
- self-correction vs user-directed correction: Self-correction means the AI caught its OWN mistake unprompted. If the user said "that's wrong" or "try a different approach," any subsequent pivot is normal interaction, NOT self-correction.

AGENCY — classify WHO drove this pattern:
- "user-driven": The user explicitly initiated this pattern (asked for a plan, requested tests, directed the investigation, specified the tool or approach).
- "ai-driven": The AI exhibited this pattern without user prompting (self-corrected, proactively ran tests, applied expertise without being asked to, independently explored the codebase).
- "collaborative": Both contributed, or the pattern emerged naturally from the interaction (user described the problem, AI chose the debugging methodology; user asked for a feature, AI chose to work incrementally).

When no canonical category fits, create a specific kebab-case category (a precise novel category is better than forcing a poor fit).`;
```

---

## Agency Field Schema

### TypeScript type change in `prompts.ts`:

In the `AnalysisResponse` interface, update the `effective_patterns` array:

```typescript
effective_patterns: Array<{
  category: string;
  description: string;
  confidence: number;
  agency?: 'user-driven' | 'ai-driven' | 'collaborative';
}>;
```

Note: `agency` is optional (`?`) during the transition period so existing data without the field does not break parsing. Once backfill is complete, it can be made required.

### JSON format template change in `generateSessionAnalysisPrompt`:

```json
"effective_patterns": [
  {
    "category": "structured-planning",
    "description": "Broke the migration into 3 phases: schema change, data backfill, API update",
    "confidence": 85,
    "agency": "user-driven"
  }
]
```

### Facet-only prompt JSON format change in `generateFacetOnlyPrompt`:

```json
"effective_patterns": [
  { "category": "kebab-case-category", "description": "technique", "confidence": 85, "agency": "user-driven" }
]
```

### Outdated detection:

Add to the existing UNION query that flags sessions needing backfill:

```sql
-- Sessions with effective_patterns missing agency field
SELECT sf.session_id
FROM session_facets sf
WHERE sf.effective_patterns IS NOT NULL
  AND sf.effective_patterns != '[]'
  AND NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM json_each(sf.effective_patterns) AS ep
    WHERE json_extract(ep.value, '$.agency') IS NOT NULL
  )
```
